title: Page 7 letter 11 and 14 — proofreading-marker note
html:
TL;DR

  • Empty-body Apple Note from October 23, 2018 — title-only proofreading marker: "Page 7 letter 11 and 14, forgot a in personal and r in our."
  • Yahir at 15 logging two specific typos (the missing a in personal, the missing r in our) on page 7, characters 11 and 14, of an unspecified document — likely a college prep or scholarship essay (the Al Forthan / JCEF / Ford essay-cluster era is just beginning).
  • Earliest extant note as letter to self in the corpus that uses Apple Notes as a correction-buffer — the same junk-drawer holding the typo-coordinates as it later holds dream-logs and dad-quotes.
  • The form pre-dates Google Docs comments / track-changes for Yahir's revision habit — typos get caught on phone, logged, then fixed at desktop.
  • One of the very first dated notes in the entire vault corpus (October 2018) — a baseline for "what was Yahir using Apple Notes for at the start" (answer: small mechanical reminders to himself).

Note

Fifteen words. The note records page 7, characters 11 and 14 — coordinate-precise — of a missing a and missing r. The document is unnamed, lost to time; only the typo-coordinates survive. This is Yahir's in its purest mechanical mode: capture the correction now, fix it later, never name what you're fixing.

The typos themselves are diagnostic — personal missing its a and our missing its r are small-vowel-drop errors typical of fast-typing teenagers, not handwritten errors. So Yahir was likely proofreading a printed copy of something he typed earlier — the workflow that recurs across the application essay season. Worth keeping as a teen era timestamp: as of October 2018, Yahir is already using Apple Notes as his second brain, even if the contents are this small.

Connections

  • — form
  • — storage form
  • college admissions — likely document context
  • grants pass teen era — period
